title: Staging Changes
subtitle: Choosing what to save

sections.instruction: This project has two files but git isn't tracking anything yet. First, initialize git in this folder.

onSuccess.message: Repository initialized. Now let's tell git which files to track.

sections.instruction: Staging is how you choose which changes to include in your next save point. Think of it like packing items into a box before shipping.
sections.prompt: Type `git add index.html` to stage that file.

onSuccess.message: Staged! `index.html` is now in the 'staging area' — ready to be saved. Think of staging like packing items into a box before shipping.

sections.instruction: Let's check the status to see the difference between staged and unstaged files.
sections.prompt: Type `git status` to see what changed.

onSuccess.message: `index.html` is now under 'Changes to be committed' — it's staged and ready. But `style.css` is still listed as 'untracked'. It hasn't been added yet.

sections.instruction: You can stage everything at once instead of adding files one by one.
sections.prompt: Type `git add .` to stage all files. The `.` means 'everything in this folder'.

onSuccess.message: The `.` means 'everything in this folder'. Both `index.html` and `style.css` are now staged and ready to be saved in your next commit.

completionMessage: You learned `git add` to stage specific files and `git add .` to stage everything. Staging lets you choose exactly what goes into each save point. Next: actually saving those changes.
